pattern: Every pair among the n final unit pieces gets separated by exactly one split somewhere in the process, and that split contributes exactly 1 to the a*b product for that pair at the moment it happens, so the total cost is always the same fixed count of pairs no matter how the splitting is done — no search over split strategies is actually needed.
hints: Try computing the minimum cost for small n by hand with a couple of different splitting strategies (e.g. splitting 4 as 1+3 vs as 2+2) and compare the totals — notice they come out the same either way.

Think of the n final 1s as n distinct tokens. Every single split partitions the current group into two groups and costs (size of group A) * (size of group B), which counts exactly one unit of 'cost' for every pair of tokens that end up on opposite sides of that particular split.

Every pair of the n tokens ends up on opposite sides of exactly one split during the whole process (the split where their paths first diverge), so the total cost, summed over the entire process, is always exactly the number of pairs of n tokens: n * (n - 1) / 2.

edge_inputs.description: n=1 — already a single one, no splits needed
n=3 — verifies the formula against the statement's own worked example
n=4 — splitting as 1+3 vs 2+2 gives different-looking intermediate costs that must still sum to the same total
n=499 — one below the constraint ceiling
n=500 — the constraint's exact upper bound

stress.description: random n across the full domain, seeded
